summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Importance of Financial Literacy
summary_sections.content_html:
Importance of Financial Literacy

Financial literacy is the ability to understand and effectively manage personal finances. It includes budgeting, investing, debt management, and planning for the future.

Why It Matters

  • ~56% of Americans cannot cover a $1,000 emergency expense from savings.
  • Average US household credit card debt: ~$6,500.
  • Financial stress is the #1 source of stress for adults.
  • Financially literate individuals save more, invest better, and retire earlier.

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Sources of Income (wages, salaries, investments)
summary_sections.content_html:
Sources of Income

Types

  • Earned income: Wages (hourly), salaries (annual), tips, commissions, bonuses.
  • Investment income: Dividends, interest, capital gains, rental income.
  • Passive income: Income requiring minimal ongoing effort (royalties, rental, business ownership).
  • Transfer payments: Government benefits (Social Security, unemployment, disability).

Gross vs. Net Income

Gross income = total earnings before deductions. Net income = take-home pay after taxes, insurance, retirement contributions.

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Budgeting Basics
summary_sections.content_html:
Budgeting Basics

A budget is a plan for how to allocate income to expenses, savings, and investments.

The 50/30/20 Rule

  • 50% Needs: housing, food, utilities, insurance, minimum debt payments.
  • 30% Wants: entertainment, dining out, hobbies, subscriptions.
  • 20% Savings/Debt repayment: emergency fund, retirement, extra debt payments.

Zero-Based Budgeting

Every dollar is assigned a purpose: Income − All allocations = $0. Forces intentional spending.

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Needs vs. Wants
summary_sections.content_html:
Needs vs. Wants

Needs are essential for survival and basic functioning: food, shelter, clothing, healthcare, transportation to work.

Wants are things that improve quality of life but aren't essential: dining out, streaming services, designer clothing, vacations.

Gray Areas

Many expenses fall between needs and wants. A smartphone may be a need (for work) but the latest model is a want. Basic transportation is a need; a luxury car is a want.

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Opportunity Cost in Financial Decisions
summary_sections.content_html:
Opportunity Cost

Opportunity cost is the value of the best alternative forgone when making a choice. Every financial decision has an opportunity cost.

Examples

  • Spending $5,000 on vacation vs. investing it (at 8% for 30 years: ~$50,000).
  • Attending college: tuition + 4 years of potential wages vs. higher lifetime earnings.
  • Buying a car with cash vs. investing and financing at low interest.

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Short-Term vs. Long-Term Goals
summary_sections.content_html:
Financial Goals

Short-Term Goals (< 1 year)

  • Build emergency fund ($500–$1,000 starter).
  • Pay off a specific credit card.
  • Save for a vacation or purchase.

Medium-Term Goals (1–5 years)

  • Build full emergency fund (3–6 months expenses).
  • Save for down payment on house or car.
  • Pay off student loans.

Long-Term Goals (5+ years)

  • Retirement savings (start early: compound interest!).
  • College fund for children.
  • Achieve financial independence.

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Case Studies in Personal Finance
summary_sections.content_html:
Case Studies in Personal Finance

Case 1: The Power of Starting Early

Investor A saves $200/month from age 22–32 (10 years, $24,000 total) then stops. Investor B saves $200/month from age 32–62 (30 years, $72,000 total). At 8% return, Investor A has MORE at 62 (~$470,000 vs. ~$300,000) despite investing less, because of compound interest and time.

Case 2: Debt Spiral

$5,000 credit card balance at 20% APR, paying only minimums: takes ~20 years to pay off, total paid: ~$12,000 (more than double the original debt).

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Financial Decision-Making Models
summary_sections.content_html:
Financial Decision-Making Models

Rational Decision-Making Process

  • Step 1: Identify the decision (e.g., buy vs. lease a car).
  • Step 2: Gather information (costs, terms, alternatives).
  • Step 3: Identify alternatives and their opportunity costs.
  • Step 4: Evaluate using criteria (cost, risk, time, alignment with goals).
  • Step 5: Make and implement the decision.
  • Step 6: Review and learn from the outcome.

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Simple Interest Formula
summary_sections.content_html:
Simple Interest

Simple interest is calculated only on the original principal.

Formula

I = P × r × t

  • I = interest earned (or owed)
  • P = principal (initial amount)
  • r = annual interest rate (as decimal)
  • t = time in years

Total amount: A = P + I = P(1 + rt)

Example: $1,000 at 5% for 3 years → I = $1,000 × 0.05 × 3 = $150. Total = $1,150.

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Compound Interest Formula
summary_sections.content_html:
Compound Interest

Compound interest is calculated on the principal plus accumulated interest.

Formula

A = P(1 + r/n)^(nt)

  • A = final amount
  • P = principal
  • r = annual rate (decimal)
  • n = compounding periods per year
  • t = time in years

Example: $1,000 at 5% compounded monthly for 3 years → A = 1000(1 + 0.05/12)^(12×3) = $1,161.62

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Continuous Compounding
summary_sections.content_html:
Continuous Compounding

When interest compounds infinitely often, we use the continuous compounding formula:

A = Pe^(rt)

  • e ≈ 2.71828 (Euler's number)
  • This is the mathematical limit of (1 + r/n)^(nt) as n → ∞

Example: $1,000 at 5% continuously for 3 years → A = 1000e^(0.05×3) = 1000e^0.15 = $1,161.83

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Growth vs. Decay Models
summary_sections.content_html:
Exponential Growth & Decay in Finance

Growth: A = P(1 + r)^t

Investments, savings, economic growth — amount increases over time.

Decay: A = P(1 − r)^t

Depreciation, inflation erosion, declining values — amount decreases over time.

Example (depreciation): Car purchased for $30,000 depreciating at 15%/year → After 5 years: 30000(1−0.15)^5 = $13,312

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Applications in Savings Accounts
summary_sections.content_html:
Savings Account Applications

Types of Savings Vehicles

  • Regular savings: Low interest (~0.01–0.5% at traditional banks).
  • High-yield savings: ~4–5% APY (online banks, 2024).
  • Certificates of Deposit (CDs): Fixed rate for fixed term; higher rate for longer terms.
  • Money market accounts: Higher rates than regular savings; may have check-writing privileges.

FDIC Insurance

US bank deposits insured up to $250,000 per depositor per institution.

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Comparing Interest Scenarios
summary_sections.content_html:
Comparing Interest Scenarios

Key Comparisons

  • Simple vs. compound: $10,000 at 5% for 20 years: Simple = $20,000; Compound (annual) = $26,533; Compound (monthly) = $27,126.
  • Different rates: $10,000 for 30 years: at 6% = $57,435; at 8% = $100,627; at 10% = $174,494.
  • Starting early vs. late: $100/month at 8% for 40 years = $349,101; for 20 years = $58,902.

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Case Studies in Banking
summary_sections.content_html:
Case Studies in Banking

Case 1: Traditional vs. Online Banking

Sarah has $25,000 in emergency savings. Traditional bank: 0.05% APY = $12.50/year. Online high-yield: 4.5% APY = $1,125/year. Difference: $1,112/year for the same FDIC-insured money.

Case 2: CD Strategy

Mike uses a CD ladder: $10,000 split into 5 CDs (1-5 year terms). As each matures, he reinvests at the longest term. Balances access with higher rates.

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Technology Tools for Interest Calculations
summary_sections.content_html:
Technology for Interest Calculations

Tools

  • Spreadsheets (Excel/Sheets): FV(), PV(), RATE(), NPER(), PMT() functions built-in.
  • Financial calculators: TI BA II Plus, HP 12C — input N, I/Y, PV, PMT, FV.
  • Online calculators: Bankrate, NerdWallet, Investor.gov compound interest calculators.
  • Programming: Python, JavaScript for custom financial modeling.

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Loan Terminology & Concepts
summary_sections.content_html:
Loan Fundamentals

  • Principal: Amount borrowed.
  • Interest rate: Cost of borrowing, expressed as APR (Annual Percentage Rate).
  • Term: Loan duration (e.g., 30-year mortgage, 5-year auto loan).
  • Collateral: Asset pledged to secure a loan (home for mortgage, car for auto).
  • Secured vs. unsecured: Secured has collateral (lower rate); unsecured does not (higher rate).

Key Concepts

  • APR: Total annual cost of borrowing including fees.
  • Down payment: Upfront portion paid to reduce principal borrowed.
  • Amortization: Gradual repayment through scheduled payments.

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Amortization Schedules
summary_sections.content_html:
Amortization Schedules

An amortization schedule shows the breakdown of each payment into principal and interest.

Monthly Payment Formula

PMT = P × r(1+r)^n / [(1+r)^n − 1]

  • P = loan principal
  • r = monthly rate (annual rate ÷ 12)
  • n = total number of monthly payments

Early payments are mostly interest; later payments are mostly principal.

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Mortgage Calculations
summary_sections.content_html:
Mortgage Calculations

Key Numbers

  • Home price − down payment = loan amount
  • Total monthly cost = PITI (Principal + Interest + Taxes + Insurance)
  • Affordability rule: Housing should be ≤ 28% of gross monthly income.

Example

Home: $350,000. Down: $70,000 (20%). Loan: $280,000 at 6.5% for 30 years.

Monthly P&I: ~$1,770. Taxes: ~$365. Insurance: ~$150. Total PITI: ~$2,285.

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Credit Card Mathematics
summary_sections.content_html:
Credit Card Mathematics

Key Features

  • APR: Typical credit card APR ranges from 15–25%.
  • Daily rate: APR ÷ 365 (used for daily balance calculations).
  • Minimum payment: Often 1–3% of balance or $25 (whichever is greater).
  • Grace period: ~25 days to pay in full before interest accrues on purchases.

Example: $5,000 balance at 20% APR with minimum payments only → takes ~25 years and costs ~$8,400 in interest!

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Student Loan Analysis
summary_sections.content_html:
Student Loan Analysis

Types

  • Federal subsidized: Government pays interest while in school.
  • Federal unsubsidized: Interest accrues while in school.
  • Private loans: From banks/lenders; usually higher rates, fewer protections.

Repayment Plans

  • Standard (10-year): Fixed payments, least total interest.
  • Income-driven: Payments based on income (IBR, PAYE, REPAYE).
  • Extended (25-year): Lower payments but more total interest.

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Real Estate & Auto Loan Math
summary_sections.content_html:
Real Estate & Auto Loan Math

Auto Loans

  • Typical terms: 36, 48, 60, or 72 months.
  • New car: ~5–7% APR; Used car: ~7–10% APR (2024).
  • Cars depreciate ~20% in year 1, ~60% over 5 years.

Real Estate Investment

  • Rental income − expenses = cash flow
  • Cap rate = Net Operating Income / Property Value
  • ROI considers appreciation, cash flow, tax benefits, and equity building.

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Case Studies in Debt Management
summary_sections.content_html:
Case Studies in Debt Management

Case 1: Debt Avalanche

Maria has 3 debts: Credit card ($5,000 at 22%), Student loan ($20,000 at 6%), Car loan ($15,000 at 5%). Extra $300/month. Avalanche: Pay minimums on all; put extra toward 22% card first. Saves the most interest.

Case 2: Debt Consolidation

James consolidates $25,000 in credit card debt at 20% average into a personal loan at 10%. Monthly savings: ~$200. Total savings: ~$8,000.

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Loan Comparison & Decision-Making
summary_sections.content_html:
Loan Comparison & Decision-Making

Comparing Loan Offers

  • Compare total cost: Not just monthly payment — calculate total payments over life of loan.
  • APR vs. interest rate: APR includes fees; same interest rate can have different APRs.
  • Opportunity cost: Could the money earn more invested than the interest saved?

Decision Framework

1. Calculate total cost of each option. 2. Consider flexibility and risks. 3. Account for opportunity costs. 4. Choose based on complete analysis.

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Stocks, Bonds, & Mutual Funds
summary_sections.content_html:
Core Investment Types

Stocks

  • Shares of ownership in a company.
  • Returns: dividends + capital gains (price increase).
  • Higher risk, higher potential return.

Bonds

  • Loans to a government or corporation.
  • Returns: regular interest (coupon) payments + face value at maturity.
  • Lower risk, lower return.

Mutual Funds & ETFs

  • Pooled investments holding many stocks/bonds.
  • Instant diversification; managed (mutual funds) or passive (index ETFs).

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Risk vs. Return
summary_sections.content_html:
Risk vs. Return

Core Principle

Higher potential returns come with higher risk. The key is finding the right balance for your situation.

Risk Spectrum

  • Low risk: Savings accounts, CDs, T-bills (1–5% return).
  • Medium risk: Bonds, balanced funds (4–8% return).
  • High risk: Stocks, real estate, crypto (8–12%+ return, with volatility).

Measuring Risk

Standard deviation measures how much returns vary from the average.

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Annuities & Pension Calculations
summary_sections.content_html:
Annuities

Future Value of an Annuity

FV = PMT × [(1+r)^n − 1] / r

Calculates what regular payments grow to over time.

Present Value of an Annuity

PV = PMT × [1 − (1+r)^(−n)] / r

Calculates the lump sum value of a stream of future payments.

Example

$500/month at 7%/12 for 30 years: FV = 500 × [(1.00583)^360 − 1]/0.00583 ≈ $566,765

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Retirement Account Planning
summary_sections.content_html:
Retirement Account Planning

Account Types

  • 401(k)/403(b): Employer-sponsored; pre-tax contributions; employer match = free money.
  • Traditional IRA: Tax-deductible contributions; taxed on withdrawal.
  • Roth IRA: After-tax contributions; tax-FREE growth and withdrawals.

Key Numbers (2024)

  • 401(k) max: $23,000/year ($30,500 if 50+).
  • IRA max: $7,000/year ($8,000 if 50+).
  • Full Social Security age: 67 for those born after 1960.

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Long-Term Investment Strategies
summary_sections.content_html:
Long-Term Investment Strategies

Core Strategies

  • Buy and hold: Invest consistently; ignore short-term fluctuations.
  • Dollar-cost averaging: Fixed amount invested regularly captures market dips.
  • Rebalancing: Periodically adjust portfolio to maintain target allocation.
  • Tax-loss harvesting: Sell losers to offset gains and reduce tax burden.

Key insight: Time in the market beats timing the market.

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Diversification & Portfolio Theory
summary_sections.content_html:
Diversification & Portfolio Theory

Core Concepts

  • Diversification: Spreading investments across different assets to reduce risk without sacrificing return.
  • Correlation: How assets move relative to each other. Low or negative correlation provides the best diversification.
  • Modern Portfolio Theory (MPT): Optimal portfolios maximize return for a given level of risk.

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Case Studies in Investing
summary_sections.content_html:
Case Studies in Investing

Case 1: Dollar-Cost Averaging vs. Lump Sum

Alex inherits $60,000. Option A: Invest all now. Option B: $5,000/month for 12 months. Historically, lump sum wins ~66% of the time (market tends to go up), but DCA reduces regret risk.

Case 2: The Power of Starting Early

Emily saves $300/month from age 22-32 (10 years, $36,000 total). Mark saves $300/month from age 32-62 (30 years, $108,000 total). At 8%: Emily has $478,000; Mark has $440,000.

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Technology in Investment Analysis
summary_sections.content_html:
Technology in Investment Analysis

Tools

  • Robo-advisors: Automated portfolio management (Betterment, Wealthfront). Low cost, diversified.
  • Brokerage platforms: Fidelity, Schwab, Vanguard — research tools, screening, charting.
  • Spreadsheets: Custom modeling, what-if analysis, portfolio tracking.
  • Apps: Mint (budgeting), Personal Capital (net worth), Morningstar (fund research).

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Creating a Personal Budget
summary_sections.content_html:
Creating a Personal Budget

The 50/30/20 Rule

  • 50% — Needs: Housing, food, utilities, transportation, insurance.
  • 30% — Wants: Entertainment, dining out, hobbies, subscriptions.
  • 20% — Savings & debt: Retirement, emergency fund, extra debt payments.

Budget Equation

Income − Expenses = Surplus (or Deficit)

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Tracking Income & Expenses
summary_sections.content_html:
Tracking Income & Expenses

Income Sources

  • Gross income: Total earnings before deductions.
  • Net income: Take-home pay after taxes and deductions.

Expense Categories

  • Essential: Housing (25-35%), food (10-15%), transportation (10-15%), insurance/health.
  • Discretionary: Entertainment, subscriptions, dining, shopping.
  • Financial: Savings, investments, debt payments.

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Emergency Funds & Savings Goals
summary_sections.content_html:
Emergency Funds & Savings Goals

Emergency Fund

  • Target: 3–6 months of essential expenses.
  • Where: High-yield savings (liquid, FDIC-insured).
  • Purpose: Job loss, medical emergency, major car repair, home emergency.

Savings Goals

Use the formula: Monthly savings = Goal amount ÷ Months until deadline (or use FV/PV for interest).

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Short- & Long-Term Financial Planning
summary_sections.content_html:
Short- & Long-Term Financial Planning

Time Horizons

  • Short-term (0–3 years): Emergency fund, vacation, minor purchases.
  • Medium-term (3–10 years): Home down payment, car, graduate school.
  • Long-term (10+ years): Retirement, children's college, wealth building.

Planning Framework

1. Define goals. 2. Set timelines. 3. Calculate required savings. 4. Choose appropriate accounts. 5. Automate. 6. Review and adjust.

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Budget Case Studies
summary_sections.content_html:
Budget Case Studies

Case 1: Recent College Graduate

Income: $45,000/year ($3,200/month net). Budget: Rent $1,050, food $400, transport $350, loans $350, insurance $200, savings $400, discretionary $450.

Case 2: Young Family

Income: $85,000/year ($5,500/month net). Budget: Mortgage $1,650, childcare $1,200, food $600, utilities $250, insurance $350, cars $400, savings $550, discretionary $500.

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Budgeting Technology Tools
summary_sections.content_html:
Budgeting Technology Tools

Popular Tools

  • YNAB (You Need A Budget): Zero-based; every dollar assigned a job. ~$99/year.
  • Mint/Credit Karma: Free; auto-categorization from linked accounts.
  • Spreadsheets: Full customization; templates widely available.
  • Bank apps: Built-in spending trackers; round-up savings features.

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Lifestyle Choices & Financial Impact
summary_sections.content_html:
Lifestyle Choices & Financial Impact

Major Financial Decisions

  • Housing: Rent vs. buy; city vs. suburb. Can differ by $500-1,500/month.
  • Transportation: New car vs. used vs. public transit. $400-900/month range.
  • Education: Community college → university (save $30,000+) vs. 4-year private.
  • Career: High-cost city + high salary vs. low-cost area + lower salary.

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Budget Adjustments & Life Changes
summary_sections.content_html:
Budget Adjustments & Life Changes

Triggers for Budget Revision

  • Income changes: Raise, job loss, side income, retirement.
  • Life events: Marriage, baby, home purchase, health crisis.
  • Goal changes: New priorities, completed goals, shifting timelines.

Adjustment Strategy

1. Reassess income. 2. Re-prioritize goals. 3. Adjust allocations. 4. Implement and monitor.

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Understanding Income Tax
summary_sections.content_html:
Income Tax Fundamentals

How Income Tax Works

  • Gross income: All taxable income (wages, interest, dividends, etc.).
  • Adjusted Gross Income (AGI): Gross income minus above-the-line deductions.
  • Taxable income: AGI minus standard/itemized deductions and exemptions.
  • Tax owed: Calculated by applying tax brackets to taxable income.

The US uses a progressive tax system — higher income is taxed at higher rates.

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Tax Brackets & Marginal Rates
Key Concepts: Property, Sales, & Other Taxes
summary_sections.content_html:
Property, Sales, & Other Taxes

Property Tax

Based on assessed value of property. Rate varies by location (0.3% to 2.5%+).

Sales Tax

Percentage added to purchases. Varies by state (0% to 7.25%+) plus local additions.

Other Taxes

  • Estate/Inheritance tax: On transfers at death (federal exemption: $13.6M in 2024).
  • Excise tax: On specific goods (gas, alcohol, tobacco).
  • Payroll tax: FICA (Social Security + Medicare).

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Types of Insurance
summary_sections.content_html:
Types of Insurance

Core Insurance Types

  • Health insurance: Covers medical expenses (deductible, copay, coinsurance, out-of-pocket max).
  • Auto insurance: Liability, collision, comprehensive.
  • Homeowner's/Renter's: Property damage, liability, personal belongings.
  • Life insurance: Term (pure protection) vs. Whole (protection + cash value).
  • Disability insurance: Replaces income if you can't work.

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Risk Management Basics
summary_sections.content_html:
Risk Management

Risk Management Strategies

  • Avoid: Don't do the risky activity (don't own a pool).
  • Reduce: Take precautions (smoke detectors, safe driving).
  • Transfer: Insurance shifts financial risk to the insurer.
  • Retain: Accept the risk and self-insure (high deductible, emergency fund).

Expected Value

E(X) = Probability × Impact. Used to evaluate whether insurance is worthwhile.

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Tax Planning Case Studies
summary_sections.content_html:
Tax Planning Case Studies

Case 1: Traditional vs. Roth IRA

Alex (22% bracket): $6,500 Traditional IRA deduction saves $1,430 in taxes NOW. In retirement (12% bracket), withdrawals are taxed less — Traditional wins. But if Alex expects HIGHER bracket in retirement, Roth wins.

Case 2: Tax-Loss Harvesting

Beth has $10,000 gains and $4,000 losses. Net taxable gain = $6,000. At 15% capital gains rate, she saves $600 in taxes by harvesting the loss.

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Insurance in Real Life
summary_sections.content_html:
Insurance in Real Life

Case 1: Health Insurance Plan Comparison

Plan A: Premium $300/month, $1,000 deductible, 80/20 coinsurance, $5,000 max OOP.
Plan B: Premium $200/month, $3,000 deductible, 70/30 coinsurance, $8,000 max OOP.
For healthy year: Plan B saves $1,200. For major illness: Plan A saves up to $3,000.

Case 2: Life Insurance Needs

Couple with $80K income, $200K mortgage, 2 young kids. Need: ~$800K-1M coverage (10-12× income).

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Tax Software & Filing Tools
summary_sections.content_html:
Tax Software & Filing Tools

Tax Preparation Options

  • Free File: IRS Free File for income ≤ $79,000 (2024).
  • Tax software: TurboTax, H&R Block, TaxAct — guided interviews.
  • CPA/Tax professional: For complex situations (business, rental, investments).
  • DIY: Form 1040 — good for understanding the math behind software.

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Starting a Business
summary_sections.content_html:
Starting a Business

Business Formation Basics

  • Sole Proprietorship: Simplest; owner = business. Unlimited personal liability.
  • Partnership: Two+ owners share profits, losses, and liability.
  • LLC: Limited liability protection; pass-through taxation. Most popular for small businesses.
  • Corporation: Separate legal entity. C-Corp: double taxation. S-Corp: pass-through.

Start-up Costs

One-time costs (equipment, licenses, legal fees) vs. recurring costs (rent, utilities, payroll).

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Profit & Loss Analysis
summary_sections.content_html:
Profit & Loss (Income Statement)

Key Components

  • Revenue: Total income from sales.
  • Cost of Goods Sold (COGS): Direct costs of producing what was sold.
  • Gross Profit: Revenue − COGS.
  • Operating Expenses: Rent, utilities, marketing, salaries (not directly tied to production).
  • Net Profit: Revenue − COGS − Operating Expenses − Taxes.

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Break-Even Analysis
summary_sections.content_html:
Break-Even Analysis

The Break-Even Point

Break-even (units) = Fixed Costs ÷ (Price per Unit − Variable Cost per Unit)

Break-even (revenue) = Fixed Costs ÷ Contribution Margin Ratio

Graphical Representation

Total Revenue line crosses Total Cost line at the break-even point. Below = loss; above = profit.

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Small Business Financing
summary_sections.content_html:
Small Business Financing

Funding Sources

  • Bootstrapping: Self-funding from savings, revenue reinvestment.
  • Bank loans: Traditional debt with interest; requires collateral and good credit.
  • SBA loans: Government-backed; easier qualification, favorable terms.
  • Angel investors: Wealthy individuals investing for equity stake.
  • Venture capital: Firms investing large sums for significant equity in high-growth startups.
  • Crowdfunding: Small amounts from many people (Kickstarter, GoFundMe).

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Entrepreneurship Case Studies
summary_sections.content_html:
Entrepreneurship Case Studies

Case 1: Food Truck Startup

Start-up: $85,000 (truck $50K, equipment $20K, licenses/permits $5K, initial inventory $10K). Monthly fixed costs: $3,000 (parking fees, insurance, loan payment). Variable cost: $4/meal. Selling price: $12/meal. CM: $8/meal. BEP: 375 meals/month.

Case 2: Online Store

Start-up: $5,000 (website, initial inventory). Fixed: $500/month. Variable: 40% of revenue. CM ratio: 60%. BEP revenue: $833/month.

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Marketing & Finance
summary_sections.content_html:
Marketing & Finance

Marketing Budget & ROI

Marketing budget is typically 5-10% of revenue for established businesses, 15-20% for startups.

Key Marketing Metrics

  • CAC: Customer Acquisition Cost = Marketing Spend ÷ New Customers.
  • LTV: Customer Lifetime Value = Avg Purchase × Frequency × Customer Lifespan.
  • ROAS: Return on Ad Spend = Revenue from Ads ÷ Cost of Ads.
  • Conversion Rate: Purchasers ÷ Visitors × 100.

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Business Risk Management
summary_sections.content_html:
Business Risk Management

Types of Business Risk

  • Market risk: Demand changes, competition, economic downturns.
  • Financial risk: Cash flow problems, excessive debt, interest rate changes.
  • Operational risk: Supply chain disruptions, equipment failure, employee issues.
  • Legal/Regulatory risk: Lawsuits, regulation changes, compliance failures.

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Technology in Business Finance
summary_sections.content_html:
Technology in Business Finance

Essential Business Finance Tools

  • Accounting software: QuickBooks, Xero, FreshBooks — automate bookkeeping.
  • Payment processing: Square, Stripe, PayPal — accept digital payments.
  • Payroll services: Gusto, ADP — automate payroll taxes and compliance.
  • Spreadsheet modeling: Excel/Google Sheets for financial projections and analysis.

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Stock Market Basics
summary_sections.content_html:
Stock Market Basics

What Is a Stock?

A stock represents fractional ownership of a company. Shareholders earn returns through price appreciation and dividends.

Major Exchanges

  • NYSE: New York Stock Exchange — largest by market capitalization.
  • NASDAQ: Technology-heavy electronic exchange.
  • Market capitalization = Share Price × Shares Outstanding.

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Reading Financial Tables
summary_sections.content_html:
Reading Financial Tables & Statements

Stock Quote Components

  • Ticker: Abbreviated symbol (AAPL, MSFT).
  • Open/Close: Price at market open/close.
  • High/Low: Highest/lowest price during the trading day.
  • Volume: Number of shares traded.
  • P/E Ratio: Price ÷ Earnings Per Share — valuation metric.

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Market Indices & Benchmarks
summary_sections.content_html:
Market Indices & Benchmarks

Major US Indices

  • S&P 500: 500 large US companies — the most-used US market benchmark.
  • Dow Jones (DJIA): 30 large industrial companies — price-weighted.
  • NASDAQ Composite: All NASDAQ-listed stocks — tech-heavy.
  • Russell 2000: 2,000 small-cap companies.

Index Weighting

Market-cap weighted (S&P 500): larger companies have more influence. Price-weighted (DJIA): higher-priced stocks have more influence.

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Trading Strategies
summary_sections.content_html:
Trading & Investment Strategies

Approaches to the Market

  • Buy and Hold: Long-term ownership; ignoring short-term fluctuations.
  • Value Investing: Buying undervalued stocks (low P/E, below book value).
  • Growth Investing: Buying companies with high earnings growth potential.
  • Index Investing: Passive; match the market through index funds.
  • Dollar Cost Averaging: Fixed amount at regular intervals.

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Global Finance & Currency
summary_sections.content_html:
Global Finance & Currency

Foreign Exchange (Forex)

Currencies trade in pairs (USD/EUR). Exchange rates fluctuate based on supply/demand, interest rates, and economics.

Key Concepts

  • Exchange rate: Price of one currency in terms of another.
  • Appreciation/Depreciation: Currency gaining/losing value relative to another.
  • Purchasing Power Parity: Theory that exchange rates should equalize prices across countries.

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Market Case Studies
summary_sections.content_html:
Market Case Studies

Case 1: 2008 Financial Crisis

S&P 500 fell ~57% from Oct 2007 to Mar 2009. An investor who stayed invested recovered by Mar 2013 and gained ~400% by 2024. One who sold at the bottom locked in losses permanently.

Case 2: COVID Crash & Recovery (2020)

S&P 500 dropped 34% in 23 trading days (fastest bear market ever). Recovery: new highs within 5 months.

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Technology in Markets
summary_sections.content_html:
Technology in Financial Markets

Modern Trading Technology

  • Online brokerages: Fidelity, Schwab, Robinhood — $0 commission trades.
  • Robo-advisors: Betterment, Wealthfront — automated portfolio management.
  • Trading apps: Mobile access to real-time quotes, trading, and account management.
  • Algorithmic trading: Computer programs executing trades at high speed based on algorithms.

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Behavioral Finance
summary_sections.content_html:
Behavioral Finance

Common Cognitive Biases

  • Loss aversion: Pain of loss > pleasure of equal gain (2:1 ratio).
  • Confirmation bias: Seeking info that supports existing beliefs.
  • Anchoring: Over-relying on first piece of information (purchase price).
  • Overconfidence: Overestimating one's ability to pick stocks or time markets.
  • Recency bias: Overweighting recent events in predictions.

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Time Value of Money
summary_sections.content_html:
Time Value of Money (TVM)

Core Principle

A dollar today is worth more than a dollar tomorrow because it can be invested to earn a return.

Key Formulas

  • Future Value: FV = PV × (1 + r)^n
  • Present Value: PV = FV / (1 + r)^n
  • FV of Annuity: FV = PMT × [((1+r)^n − 1) / r]
  • PV of Annuity: PV = PMT × [(1 − (1+r)^−n) / r]

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Net Present Value (NPV)
summary_sections.content_html:
Net Present Value

NPV Formula

NPV = −Initial Investment + Σ (Cash Flow_t / (1+r)^t)

If NPV > 0, the investment adds value. If NPV < 0, it destroys value.

Decision Rule

Accept projects with NPV > 0. Among multiple projects, choose the highest NPV.

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Internal Rate of Return (IRR)
summary_sections.content_html:
Internal Rate of Return

IRR Definition

The discount rate that makes NPV = 0. It's the project's effective annual return.

Decision Rule

If IRR > required return (hurdle rate): accept. If IRR < hurdle rate: reject.

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Financial Modeling
summary_sections.content_html:
Financial Modeling

Building Financial Models

  • Inputs: Assumptions (growth rate, costs, discount rate).
  • Calculations: Revenue projections, expense forecasts, cash flows.
  • Outputs: NPV, IRR, break-even, financial statements.

Scenario Analysis

Best case, base case, worst case — testing how changes in assumptions affect outcomes.

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Corporate Finance Fundamentals
summary_sections.content_html:
Corporate Finance

Capital Structure

The mix of debt (loans, bonds) and equity (stock, retained earnings) used to finance a company.

Cost of Capital

  • Cost of Debt: Interest rate on borrowing × (1 − tax rate) — interest is tax-deductible.
  • Cost of Equity: Return required by shareholders (typically estimated via CAPM).
  • WACC: Weighted average of debt and equity costs.

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Risk Analysis & Quantification
summary_sections.content_html:
Risk Analysis

Measuring Risk

  • Standard Deviation: Measures volatility of returns (higher = more risk).
  • Sharpe Ratio: (Return − Risk-free Rate) / Standard Deviation — risk-adjusted return.
  • Value at Risk (VaR): Maximum expected loss at a given confidence level.

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: AP Economics & Finance Connections
summary_sections.content_html:
AP Economics & Finance Connections

Macro Concepts in Finance

  • GDP: Total economic output — impacts corporate earnings and stock market.
  • Inflation: Erodes purchasing power; affects interest rates and investment decisions.
  • Federal Reserve: Sets monetary policy (interest rates, money supply) affecting all financial markets.
  • Fiscal Policy: Government spending and taxation affecting economic growth.

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: International Finance Case Studies
summary_sections.content_html:
International Finance Case Studies

Case 1: Currency Impact on Investment Returns

A US investor buys European stock at €100 when 1 EUR = 1.10 USD (cost: $110). Stock rises 10% to €110. If EUR weakens to 1 EUR = 1.05 USD: $110 × 1.05 = $115.50 return on $110 cost = 5% return (vs. 10% in euros).

Case 2: Emerging Market Opportunities

Higher growth potential (7-8% GDP) vs. developed markets (2-3%), but with currency risk, political risk, and volatility.

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Review of Key Formulas
summary_sections.content_html:
Key Financial Math Formulas

Time Value of Money

  • FV = PV × (1 + r)^n
  • PV = FV / (1 + r)^n
  • Rule of 72: Years to double ≈ 72 / rate

Business Math

  • Break-even (units) = Fixed Costs / (Price − Variable Cost)
  • Profit Margin = Net Profit / Revenue × 100
  • ROI = (Gain − Cost) / Cost × 100

Investment Math

  • NPV = −Investment + Σ(CF_t / (1+r)^t)
  • Sharpe Ratio = (Return − Risk-free) / Std Dev
  • WACC = (% Debt × Cost of Debt × (1−Tax)) + (% Equity × Cost of Equity)

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: AP Exam Practice
summary_sections.content_html:
AP-Style Practice Problems

Multi-Step Calculations

AP problems require combining multiple concepts: TVM, tax effects, risk analysis, and economic reasoning.

Free Response Strategy

  • Show ALL work and calculations.
  • Label formulas before plugging in numbers.
  • Explain economic reasoning, not just math.

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: AP Economics Context
summary_sections.content_html:
AP Economics Connections

Microeconomics Connections

Supply/demand curves → market price → stock pricing. Elasticity → pricing strategy. Marginal analysis → optimal decisions.

Macroeconomics Connections

GDP → corporate earnings → market returns. Inflation → interest rates → bond prices. Fed policy → all financial markets.

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Real-World Applications
summary_sections.content_html:
Real-World Financial Math Applications

Everyday Decisions

  • Renting vs. Buying: Comparing total costs including opportunity cost of down payment.
  • Car Buying: Lease vs. buy analysis; total cost of ownership.
  • College ROI: Tuition cost vs. lifetime earnings increase.
  • Retirement Planning: How much to save monthly to reach a goal.

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Capstone: Personal Financial Plan
summary_sections.content_html:
Capstone: Build Your Personal Financial Plan

Plan Components

  • 1. Net Worth Statement: Assets − Liabilities = Net Worth.
  • 2. Budget: Income − Expenses = Surplus (invest) or Deficit (cut spending).
  • 3. Emergency Fund: 3-6 months expenses in savings.
  • 4. Debt Payoff Plan: Priority: high-interest first (avalanche method).
  • 5. Investment Plan: Retirement contributions, asset allocation by age.
  • 6. Insurance Review: Adequate coverage for health, auto, life, disability.
  • 7. Estate Planning: Will, beneficiary designations, power of attorney.

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Comprehensive Review
summary_sections.content_html:
Comprehensive Course Review

Course Journey

  • Units 1-2: Financial literacy fundamentals, interest, and banking.
  • Units 3-4: Loans, debt, investments, and retirement planning.
  • Units 5-6: Budgeting, taxes, and insurance.
  • Units 7-8: Business finance and stock markets.
  • Unit 9: Advanced concepts: TVM, NPV, IRR, corporate finance, risk analysis.

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: AP Financial Math Scenarios
summary_sections.content_html:
AP-Style Integrated Scenarios

Scenario: Life After Graduation

Recent graduate: $45K salary, $28K student loans at 5.5%, needs apartment, car, insurance, and retirement savings. Apply ALL course concepts to make optimal decisions.
